region_of_origin: China (Han dynasty origin, ~200 BCE); popularised in Singapore by Hokkien and Teochew immigrants
food_subgroup: Soy-based soft silken tofu pudding served with sugar syrup; Singapore hawker dessert

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Tau Huay / Douhua. FOODON_03316193 (tofu) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for silken tofu pudding with sugar syrup. A0LKF (soft tofu product) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Tau Huay or silken tofu pudding.

preparation_method: soy milk coagulated with calcium sulphate (gypsum)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Singapore hawker bowl of tau huay consists of approximately 200g of soft silken tofu pudding topped with approximately 30g of sugar syrup, totalling ~230g per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Tau Huay — HPB Singapore Hawker Dessert Nutrition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore hawker food nutrition database for Tau Huay (tofu pudding with sugar syrup). Per-100g values represent the composite dish (tofu + syrup) as served. Values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central silken tofu entries (NDB 16426) adjusted for syrup contribution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — silken tofu and sugar syrup composite estimate; University of Sydney GI Database reference for soy-based foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~55 represents the effective GI of the whole bowl WITH STANDARD SYRUP (~230g). The tau huay itself (silken tofu, no syrup) has GI ~15 per University of Sydney GI Database (soy-based foods). The composite GI of ~55 is derived from the weighted contribution of the sugar syrup (~28g sucrose, GI ~65) and the tofu base (~2g soy carbohydrate, GI ~15), yielding an effective bowl GI of approximately 55. GL per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 28.06g) / 100 ≈ 15.4. WITHOUT SYRUP: GI ~15, GL ~0.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (~30mg per bowl, ~13mg per 100g)
hypertension.guidance: Tau huay is one of the lowest-sodium hawker foods in Singapore (~30mg sodium per bowl) and poses negligible hypertension concern; the calcium sulphate coagulant contributes ~70mg calcium per 100g tofu, which may offer modest cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar syrup contribution: ~28g sucrose per bowl drives GI ~55 and GL ~15.4
type2_diabetes.guidance: With standard sugar syrup the bowl delivers GI ~55 and GL ~15.4, representing a moderate glycaemic impact for a single dessert; requesting 'no syrup' reduces GL to ~0.3 and transforms tau huay into a near-zero-GL, high-protein food suitable for tight glycaemic management in Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.2g/100g), soy isoflavones, very low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tau huay is low in saturated fat (0.2g/100g), contains soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) with Grade C evidence for modest LDL-lowering effects, and is very low in sodium (~30mg/bowl), making it a cardiovascular-friendly dessert choice when consumed without excess syrup.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones with modest anti-inflammatory properties; calcium from gypsum coagulant supports bone health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Soy isoflavones in tau huay have Grade C evidence for modest anti-inflammatory effects relevant to RA; the gypsum coagulant provides ~70mg calcium per 100g tofu, supporting bone health in RA patients at increased osteoporosis risk from corticosteroid use; the sugar syrup (28g) is pro-inflammatory at this dose and should be omitted where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tau Huay (豆花, Dòu Huā) is a traditional soft silken tofu pudding served as a hawker dessert in Singapore, with origins in Han dynasty China (~200 BCE) brought to Singapore by Hokkien and Teochew immigrants. It is made by coagulating fresh soy milk with calcium sulphate (gypsum), producing an exceptionally smooth, delicate, custard-like texture that is softer than regular tofu. Singapore's hawker version is typically served warm, topped with a plain or ginger-infused sugar syrup. Per standard bowl WITH SYRUP (~230g): ~165 kcal, 8g protein, 3g fat, 28g carbohydrate (~28g sugar), GI ~55, GL ~15.4, ~30mg sodium. WITHOUT SYRUP (~200g): ~55 kcal, 8g protein, 3g fat, 2g carbohydrate, GI ~15, GL ~0.3. The sugar syrup is the single most important clinical variable — requesting 'no syrup' transforms tau huay from a moderate-GL dessert into a near-zero-GL, high-protein food. The gypsum coagulant contributes ~140mg calcium per bowl. Tau huay is vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is available at hawker centres and dedicated tofu pudding stalls across Singapore. Source: HPB Singapore hawker nutrition database.
common_user_queries: tau huay with syrup vs without

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore hawker food nutrition database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. GI estimate is derived from University of Sydney GI Database reference values for soy-based foods and sucrose, with transparent composite calculation documented. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central silken tofu entries.
notes: Per-100g values represent the composite dish as served (tofu + sugar syrup, ~230g bowl). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for tau huay not available in HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-14. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish (tofu + syrup) not reported in HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-14. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per HPB Singapore source. cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0: confirmed zero — tau huay is a plant-based soy product containing no animal-derived cholesterol. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per HPB Singapore source for this plant-based soy dessert. CRITICAL CLINICAL NOTE: The sugar syrup is the dominant glycaemic variable. WITH SYRUP: GI ~55, GL ~15.4 (moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes). WITHOUT SYRUP: GI ~15, GL ~0.3 (negligible glycaemic impact). Ginger-infused syrup vs plain sugar syrup: identical glycaemic impact at hawker cooking quantities. The gypsum coagulant contributes approximately 140mg calcium per bowl (~70mg/100g), which is nutritionally significant for bone health.
